verses.commentary: Classical Hellenistic preface (compare Josephus, Against Apion). 'Many have undertaken' acknowledges prior accounts. 'Have been fulfilled (peplerophoremenon, πεπληροφορημένων) among us' — historical events with theological accomplishment.

Sources: 'eyewitnesses (autoptai, αὐτόπται) and servants of the word.' Apostolic tradition handed down (paredosan, παρέδοσαν). Luke is a second-generation researcher relying on first-generation witnesses.

'Having followed everything carefully from the beginning, to write to you an orderly account (kathexes, καθεξῆς), most excellent Theophilus.' Methodical, sequential, with a dedicatee — possibly a Roman patron. The Gospel is sponsored historiography.
Purpose: 'that you may know the certainty (asphaleia, ἀσφάλεια) of the things you have been taught.' Catechetical reinforcement. Faith resting on verifiable accounts.

'In the days of Herod king of Judea' — datable history. Priestly division of Abijah (1 Chronicles 24:10) — one of 24 rotational priestly courses. Elizabeth also from Aaron's line. The forerunner's lineage is priestly.
Both 'righteous (dikaioi, δίκαιοι) before God, walking blamelessly in all the commandments.' Their childlessness is not punishment for sin. Pious, faithful, suffering.
Elizabeth barren, both advanced in years. Echo of Sarah and Abraham, Hannah and Elkanah. Barrenness reversed by divine intervention is a recurring covenant motif.
Zechariah's division on duty — twice yearly. He serves as priest before God.
Chosen by lot (elache, ἔλαχε — once-in-a-lifetime privilege) to burn incense in the sanctuary. Many priests never received this lot. Singular moment.
Whole congregation praying outside at the hour of incense. The community's prayers ascend with the smoke. Liturgical setting.
Angel of the Lord appears, standing at the right side of the altar of incense. Right side — position of favor. The same place priests rarely venture is suddenly inhabited by a heavenly messenger.
Zechariah is troubled (etarachthe, ἐταράχθη) and fear falls on him. Standard human response to angelic visitation.

Angel: 'Do not fear, Zechariah. Your prayer (deesis, δέησις) has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will bear a son; call him John.' John (Yohanan, יוֹחָנָן) = 'YHWH is gracious.' The name interprets the act.
'Joy and gladness' for Zechariah; many will rejoice at his birth. The forerunner generates joy in his very arrival.
'Great before the Lord. Will drink no wine or strong drink' — Nazirite vow (Numbers 6). 'Filled with the Holy Spirit even from his mother's womb.' Pneumatic from gestation.
Will turn many of Israel's children back to the Lord their God. Restoration of covenant fidelity is his mission.

Goes before the Lord 'in the spirit and power of Elijah' (Malachi 4:5-6). Hearts of fathers to children, disobedient to wisdom of righteous. Prepares a people for the Lord. Elijah-typology made explicit.
Zechariah: 'How shall I know this? I am an old man.' Comparable to Abraham's question (Genesis 15:8) but without faith's framing. Doubt voiced.
'I am Gabriel, who stands in the presence of God' (compare Daniel 8:16; 9:21). The angel identifies himself by rank. Sent to bring this good news (euangelisasthai, εὐαγγελίσασθαι).
Sign of judgment-grace: silent until the day this happens. Disbelief silenced. Gabriel does not punish without provision; the silence is also incubation for praise (1:64).
People wait, wondering at his delay in the temple. Zechariah's lingering causes confusion.
Comes out unable to speak. They realize he saw a vision. Communicates by signs. The priestly blessing he should give cannot be spoken.
Time of service ended, he goes home. Silent priest carries the news in his body.
Elizabeth conceives, hides herself five months. Privacy in pregnancy. Cultural concealment of early pregnancy plus reverent solitude.
'The Lord has done this for me — taken away my reproach (oneidos, ὄνειδος) among my people.' Barrenness was social stigma; conception removes it. Theological framing of personal reversal.
Sixth month — Gabriel sent again, this time to Nazareth in Galilee. Geographic and social step down from Jerusalem temple.

Virgin (parthenon, παρθένον) betrothed to a man named Joseph, of David's house. Virgin's name: Mary (Mariam, Μαριάμ). Davidic line maintained through Joseph; the miracle of conception will preserve the line through Mary's body.

Angel enters: 'Greetings, favored one (kecharitomene, κεχαριτωμένη — perfect passive participle, one having been graced). The Lord is with you.' Grace already bestowed. Mary does not earn favor; she receives it.
Mary troubled at the saying, considering what kind of greeting this is. She thinks rather than panics. Reflective response.
'Do not fear, Mary, you have found grace (charis, χάρις) with God.' The angel calms before announcing.
'Conceive in your womb and bear a son; call his name Jesus (Iesous, Ἰησοῦς — Yeshua, YHWH saves).' The name carries the mission, as in Matthew 1:21.
'Great — called Son of the Most High. Lord God will give him the throne of his father David.' Davidic covenant fulfillment (2 Samuel 7:12-13).
'Reign over the house of Jacob forever; his kingdom will have no end.' Eternal kingship. Daniel 7:14 echoes.
'How will this be, since I do not know a man?' Mary's question, unlike Zechariah's, is not unbelief — she asks 'how,' not 'how shall I know.' Practical inquiry.

'Holy Spirit will come upon you; the power of the Most High will overshadow (episkiasei, ἐπισκιάσει — same word as Sinai cloud in Exodus 40:35) you. Therefore the holy one to be born will be called the Son of God.' Trinitarian conception, theophanic imagery.
'Your relative Elizabeth has also conceived in her old age — sixth month for her who was called barren.' Confirmation in the previous miracle. Mary is given a sign she did not request.
'No word from God will fail (adynatesei, ἀδυνατήσει — be powerless).' Echo of Genesis 18:14 to Sarah. The promise has divine guarantee.
'Behold the servant (doule, δούλη) of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word.' Mary's fiat. Submission as faith. The angel departs.
Mary travels with haste (spoude, σπουδῇ) to the hill country, to Judah's town. Faith mobilizes immediately.
Enters Zechariah's house, greets Elizabeth. The two miraculous mothers meet.
When Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting — the baby in her womb leaped. Elizabeth filled with the Holy Spirit. John, in utero, is already a forerunner; his recognition precedes his birth.
Elizabeth exclaims loudly: 'Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb!' Beatitude addressed to Mary; second beatitude on the child.
'Why is this granted to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?' 'My Lord' (ho kyrios mou, ὁ κύριός μου) — Elizabeth recognizes the divinity already. Echo of Psalm 110:1.
'When the sound of your greeting came, the baby leaped for joy.' Joy in the womb. Prenatal recognition.
'Blessed is she who believed there would be a fulfillment of what was spoken.' Mary's faith is honored explicitly — Elizabeth contrasts implicitly with Zechariah's unbelief.
Magnificat begins. 'My soul magnifies (megalynei, μεγαλύνει) the Lord.' First of Luke's four canticles (Magnificat, Benedictus, Gloria, Nunc Dimittis). Echoes Hannah's song (1 Samuel 2:1-10).
'My spirit rejoices in God my Savior.' Even Mary needs a Savior. The mother of the Messiah is also a recipient of his salvation.

'He has regarded the lowly state (tapeinosin, ταπείνωσιν) of his servant. From now on all generations will call me blessed.' Social reversal anticipated. Mary's low status becomes universally celebrated.
'The Mighty One has done great things for me — holy is his name.' Theological focus stays on God's action through her, not on her merit.
'His mercy is on those who fear him from generation to generation.' Covenant continuity. Fear of the Lord defines the recipients across time.
'He has shown strength with his arm; scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts.' Divine warrior imagery. Interior pride exposed and scattered.
'Brought down the mighty from their thrones; exalted those of humble estate.' Class reversal. Lukan theme inaugurated.
'Filled the hungry with good things; sent the rich away empty.' Economic reversal. Beatitudes-and-woes shape (Luke 6:20-26) anticipated here.
'Helped his servant Israel, remembering his mercy.' Covenant memory. God's loyalty to Israel framed as remembrance.
'As he spoke to our fathers — to Abraham and his offspring forever.' Abrahamic covenant fulfillment-promise. Mary's child is the embodiment of God's faithfulness.
Mary stays about three months, returns home. Mathematically aligns with Elizabeth's full term arrival; Mary may have witnessed John's birth.
Elizabeth's time arrives, gives birth to a son. The first promised miracle birth comes to pass.
Neighbors and relatives hear; the Lord 'made great his mercy' (emegalynen kyrios to eleos autou, ἐμεγάλυνεν κύριος τὸ ἔλεος αὐτοῦ — same root as Magnificat). Community joy at divine reversal.
Eighth day — circumcision. They prepare to name him Zechariah, following the father. Patrimonial naming custom.
Elizabeth: 'No — he shall be called John.' She has received the angel's instruction. Maternal authority asserted.
'No one among your relatives has this name.' Naming traditions defended. Innovation resisted.
Make signs to Zechariah for his preference. The mute father consulted.
Tablet: 'His name is John.' Estin (ἐστιν, is) — present tense affirmation, not future. The name is already settled by divine appointment.
Mouth opened immediately, tongue released. Praising God. Silence ends in worship. Discipline produces gratitude.
Fear (phobos, φόβος) falls on all neighbors. All these things spoken throughout the hill country of Judea. The miracle ripples outward.
'What then will this child be?' Universal question. 'The hand of the Lord was with him' — divine accompaniment marks him.
Zechariah filled with Holy Spirit, prophesies. The Benedictus begins.

'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel — he has visited (epeskepsato, ἐπεσκέψατο) and redeemed (lytrosin, λύτρωσιν) his people.' Visitation language — divine presence in salvation acts. Redemption — covenant theme.
'Raised up a horn of salvation in the house of his servant David.' Horn = strength (Psalm 132:17). Davidic Messiah.
'As he spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old.' Continuity with the prophetic word.
'Salvation from our enemies and from the hand of all who hate us.' Initially read politically; the Gospel will redirect the enemy-category.
'To show mercy to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant.' Memory of covenant motivating present action.
'The oath he swore to our father Abraham' — Genesis 22:16-18. Abrahamic root.
'To grant us that we, delivered from the hand of enemies, might serve him without fear.' Fearless service is the goal of deliverance.
'In holiness and righteousness before him all our days.' Moral end of salvation — a transformed life pattern.
'And you, child, will be called prophet of the Most High; you will go before the Lord to prepare his ways.' Father prophesies the son's role. Echo of 1:17.
'Knowledge of salvation by the forgiveness of sins.' Salvation = forgiveness in core definition. Not military, but moral and spiritual.

'Because of the tender mercy (splagchna eleous, σπλάγχνα ἐλέους, gut-mercy) of our God — the dayspring (anatole, ἀνατολή, rising sun) from on high will visit us.' Messianic dawn imagery (Malachi 4:2).
'To shine on those in darkness and the shadow of death; to guide our feet into the way of peace.' Isaiah 9:2 echoed. Light, peace — eschatological gifts.
Child grew, became strong in spirit. Lived in the wilderness until the day of his public appearance (anadeixis, ἀνάδειξις) to Israel. Quiet formation; eventual public emergence.
